problem_statement.Title: Top-level module lacks comparable version object (version_info) — only __version__ string exposed
problem_statement.Description: Matplotlib's top-level module only exposes __version__ (a string). There is no easily comparable top-level representation (e.g., a version_info tuple or comparable version object) accessible as matplotlib.version_info, which makes programmatic version comparisons inconvenient.
problem_statement.RootCause: 1. Missing exported comparable version attribute: lib/matplotlib/__init__.py does not define or expose a version_info attribute at module level; it only provides the __version__ string. 2. Single-place lazy computation: __version__ is computed lazily inside the module-level __getattr__ and cached as a global there; no analogous logic exists to compute/cach e a numeric/tuple representation (version_info) derived from __version__. Location: lib/matplotlib/__init__.py, function __getattr__. 3. Incorrect assumption about consumers: the codebase assumes that consumers wanting to compare versions can parse the __version__ string themselves; there is no convenience API. This makes consumers implement ad-hoc parsing/comparisons. Location: top-level API (absence encoded in lib/matplotlib/__init__.py). 4. If a hardcoded convenience value were present, it would need to match the numeric release tuple derived from the parsed __version__; because no such hardcoded value exists, the correct relationship is that version_info should be computed from __version__ (e.g., version_info == tuple(parse_version(__version__).release) when available, otherwise derived by extracting numeric components via regex, and as a last resort a single-element tuple containing the raw version string).
StepsToReproduce: In Python REPL or script, import matplotlib: import matplotlib as mpl
Attempt to access a comparable version attribute: print(hasattr(mpl, 'version_info'))
Observe the attribute is not present (False) and only mpl.__version__ (string) is available
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The top-level matplotlib namespace provides a readily comparable version representation (e.g., matplotlib.version_info), so users can directly compare versions like: if matplotlib.version_info >= (3, 5): ...
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Only matplotlib.__version__ (a string) is present. There is no matplotlib.version_info attribute available for direct tuple-style comparisons.

hints_text:
It seems that `__version_info__` is the way to go.

### Prior art
- There's no official specification for version tuples. [PEP 396 - Module Version Numbers](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0396/) only defines the string `__version__`.

- Many projects don't bother with version tuples.

- When they do, `__version_info__` seems to be a common thing:
  - [Stackoverflow discussion](https://stackoverflow.com/a/466694)
  - [PySide2](https://doc.qt.io/qtforpython-5.12/pysideversion.html#printing-project-and-qt-version) uses it.

- Python itself has the string [sys.version](https://docs.python.org/3/library/sys.html#sys.version) and the (named)tuple [sys.version_info](https://docs.python.org/3/library/sys.html#sys.version_info). In analogy to that `__version_info__` next to `__version__` makes sense for packages.

problem_statement.Title: RangeSlider.set_val IndexError when polygon has 4 vertices (cannot initialize RangeSlider)
problem_statement.Description: Creating a RangeSlider with valinit triggers an IndexError in RangeSlider.set_val because the implementation assumes the polygon (self.poly) has 5 vertices and writes to xy[4], but in some environments self.poly.xy has only 4 vertices.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) RangeSlider.set_val in lib/matplotlib/widgets.py assumes self.poly.xy has 5 vertices and assigns to xy[4]. Location: RangeSlider.set_val (widgets.py). The code uses index-based in-place mutation of the polygon vertex array and expects a closing vertex to already be present.
2) The assumption that self.poly.xy always contains the appended closing vertex is incorrect. The polygon returned by axvspan/axhspan may be represented with 4 vertices (the rectangle corners) or with 5 vertices (the closing first vertex repeated). The correct relationship is that the method should consider the 4 core rectangle vertices (ordered corners) and not rely on the presence of a fifth element; constructing the 4 core vertices and assigning them to self.poly.xy is correct because Polygon.set_xy will append the closing vertex if the Polygon is configured to be closed.
3) The buggy code mutates indices up to 4 (xy[4]) which is only valid if poly.xy already contains the closing point; when poly.xy has shape (4,2) this causes IndexError: index 4 is out of bounds. Location: lib/matplotlib/widgets.py: RangeSlider.set_val (the block that sets xy[0]..xy[4]).
StepsToReproduce: Run the provided reproduction script (the one creating an image, histogram, and a RangeSlider with valinit=[0.0, 0.0]).
Observe the IndexError raised during RangeSlider construction (stack trace points to widgets.py RangeSlider.set_val and xy[4]).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: RangeSlider should accept and apply the provided initial value (valinit) and render the rectangular span on creation without raising an IndexError, updating the image/histogram accordingly.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Creating RangeSlider with valinit triggers an IndexError: 'index 4 is out of bounds for axis 0 with size 4' coming from RangeSlider.set_val when it attempts to write to xy[4].

problem_statement.Title: Hovering over image with BoundaryNorm crashes (ValueError: BoundaryNorm is not invertible)
problem_statement.Description: When an AxesImage / ScalarMappable is given a BoundaryNorm, moving the mouse over the image triggers Artist.format_cursor_data which calls the norm's inverse; BoundaryNorm.inverse raises ValueError and the exception is unhandled, causing the interactive cursor behavior to crash.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) lib/matplotlib/artist.py::Artist.format_cursor_data assumes the norm is invertible and unconditionally calls:
   neighbors = self.norm.inverse((int(self.norm(data) * n) + np.array([0, 1])) / n)
   The subsequent logic computes
   delta = abs(neighbors - data).max()
   g_sig_digits = cbook._g_sig_digits(data, delta)
   This call-site does not catch ValueError and therefore lets any ValueError from norm.inverse propagate.

2) lib/matplotlib/colors.py::BoundaryNorm.inverse intentionally raises ValueError("BoundaryNorm is not invertible"). BoundaryNorm therefore does not provide an inverse; the contract is explicit in this implementation.

3) Because format_cursor_data relies on norm.inverse only to derive neighboring data-space values (to compute a delta for digit formatting), the unhandled ValueError from a non-invertible norm (BoundaryNorm) causes interaction to stop rather than falling back to a safe formatting strategy.

4) The rest of the codebase (e.g., colorbar) already special-cases BoundaryNorm, indicating the assumption of invertibility is not globally valid; the failing assumption is localized to Artist.format_cursor_data.
StepsToReproduce:
Run the following Python script with an interactive Matplotlib backend (e.g. Qt5Agg):

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import matplotlib as mpl

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
norm = mpl.colors.BoundaryNorm(np.linspace(-4, 4, 5), 256)
X = np.random.randn(10, 10)
pc = ax.imshow(X, cmap='RdBu_r', norm=norm)
plt.show()

Move the mouse cursor over the image and observe the crash.
Alternatively, create an AxesImage/ScalarMappable with a BoundaryNorm, then hover the cursor over the image in an interactive session.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Hovering the mouse over the image shows a formatted scalar value (with an appropriate number of significant digits) in the status/toolbar without raising an exception or stopping interaction.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Hovering the mouse over the image raises ValueError: "BoundaryNorm is not invertible" (propagating from BoundaryNorm.inverse) originating from Artist.format_cursor_data -> self.norm.inverse(...), causing the interactive cursor behavior to stop.

After thinking about my suggestion again, I now understand why you prefer an `isinstace` check.
(In my initial suggestion one would have to check if `ends` maps to (0,1) and if `normed` is in [0, 1] for the correct way to implement my idea. But these conditions are taylored to catch `BoundaryNorm` anyway, so why not do it directly.)

However, I suggest using a try block after https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/blob/c33557d120eefe3148ebfcf2e758ff2357966000/lib/matplotlib/artist.py#L1305
```python
# Midpoints of neighboring color intervals.
try:
    neighbors = self.norm.inverse(
        (int(normed * n) + np.array([0, 1])) / n)
except ValueError:
    # non-invertible ScalarMappable
```
because `BoundaryNorm` raises a `ValueError` anyway and I assume this to be the case for all non-invertible `ScalarMappables`.
(And the issue is the exception raised from `inverse`).

The question is:
What to put in the `except`? So what is "acting accordingly" in this case?
If `BoundaryNorm` isn't invertible, how can we reliably get the data values of the midpoints of neighboring colors?

I think it should be enough to get the boundaries themselves, instead of the midpoints (though both is possible) with something like:
```python
cur_idx = np.argmin(np.abs(self.norm.boundaries - data))
cur_bound = self.norm.boundaries[cur_idx]
neigh_idx = cur_idx + 1 if data > cur_bound else cur_idx - 1
neighbors = self.norm.boundaries[
    np.array([cur_idx, neigh_idx])
]
```
Problems with this code are:
- `boundaries` property is specific to `BoundaryNorm`, isn't it? So we gained nothing by using `try .. except`
- more checks are needed to cover all cases for `clip` and `extend` options of `BoundaryNorm` such that `neigh_idx` is always in bounds
- for very coarse boundaries compared to data (ie: `boundaries = [0,1,2,3,4,5]; data = random(n)*5) the displayed values are always rounded to one significant digit. While in principle, this is expected (I believe), it results in inconsistency. In my example 0.111111 would be given as 0.1 and 0.001 as 0.001 and 1.234 would be just 1.
> boundaries property is specific to BoundaryNorm, isn't it? So we gained nothing by using try .. except

This is one argument in favor of doing the `isinstance` check because then you can assert we _have_ a BoundaryNorm and can trust that you can access its state etc.   One on hand, duck typeing is in general a Good Thing in Python and we should err on the side of being forgiving on input, but sometimes the complexity of it is more trouble than it is worth if you really only have 1 case that you are trying catch!  

>  I assume this to be the case for all non-invertible ScalarMappables.

However we only (at this point) are talking about a way to recover in the case of BoundaryNorm.  Let everything else continue to fail and we will deal with those issues if/when they get reported.

problem_statement.Title: matplotlib.get_backend() can clear Gcf.figs when a figure was created inside rc_context
problem_statement.Description: Calling matplotlib.get_backend() can remove existing figures from the global figure manager (plt._pylab_helpers.Gcf.figs) when the first figure in Gcf.figs was created inside an rc_context() block.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) rc_context() restores rcParams from a snapshot taken at context entry. Location: lib/matplotlib/__init__.py, function rc_context. Implementation captures orig = rcParams.copy() at entry and then does dict.update(rcParams, orig) in the finally block to revert settings. Because orig was taken before any backend resolution inside the context, this can restore the 'backend' rcParam back to the auto-backend sentinel (rcsetup._auto_backend_sentinel).

2) get_backend() simply returns rcParams['backend'], and RcParams.__getitem__ lazily resolves the auto-backend sentinel by calling pyplot.switch_backend(...). Location: lib/matplotlib/__init__.py, function get_backend and class RcParams.__getitem__. The exact behavior: if dict.__getitem__(rcParams, 'backend') is rcsetup._auto_backend_sentinel, RcParams.__getitem__ imports matplotlib.pyplot and calls plt.switch_backend(rcsetup._auto_backend_sentinel).

3) switch_backend(...) begins by closing all figures. Location: lib/matplotlib/pyplot.py, function switch_backend (and close('all')). The sequence: plt.switch_backend(...) calls close("all"), which calls _pylab_helpers.Gcf.destroy_all(), which clears the OrderedDict of managers (Gcf.figs). Therefore resolving the sentinel via get_backend() can cause all figures to be destroyed.

4) Interaction that triggers the bug (relationship preserved):
   - orig = rcParams.copy() (rc_context entry)
   - inside context: creating a figure causes backend resolution and sets rcParams['backend'] to a concrete backend
   - exiting context: dict.update(rcParams, orig) restores 'backend' to rcsetup._auto_backend_sentinel (because orig captured the sentinel)
   - later: get_backend() -> rcParams['backend'] is sentinel -> RcParams.__getitem__ calls plt.switch_backend(...) -> switch_backend calls close("all") -> Gcf.figs cleared.

5) The same snapshot/restore pattern appears in pyplot._xkcd and similarly reverts rcParams using dict.update(rcParams, self._orig), so it is also susceptible. Location: lib/matplotlib/pyplot.py, class _xkcd.__exit__.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Calling matplotlib.get_backend() should not remove or destroy figures that were already created and managed by the global figure manager (Gcf). Figures created inside an rc_context should remain in Gcf.figs after leaving the context and after calling get_backend().
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: If the first (or only) figure in Gcf.figs was created inside an rc_context, calling matplotlib.get_backend() can result in Gcf.figs becoming empty (figures are removed). Consequences: operations that expect the figure to be managed (e.g., plt.close(fig)) may fail because the figure manager has been destroyed/removed from Gcf.

hints_text:
My knee-jerk guess is that  :  

 - the `rcParams['backend']` in the auto-sentinel
 - that is stashed by rc_context
 - if you do the first thing to force the backend to be resolved in the context manager it get changes
 - the context manager sets it back to the sentinel an the way out
 - `get_backend()` re-resolves the backend which because it changes the backend it closes all of the figures


This is probably been a long standing latent bug, but was brought to the front when we made the backend resolution lazier.

problem_statement.Title: Axes3D ignores set_visible(False) — 3D axes draw methods do not honor visibility flag
problem_statement.Description: When using subplot(projection='3d'), calling set_visible(False) on the Axes instance does not hide the 3D subplot; the axes and its contents are still rendered.
problem_statement.RootCause: 1) mpl_toolkits/mplot3d/axes3d.py: Axes3D.draw — This method overrides the base Axes.draw but does not check the Artist visibility flag (self.get_visible()) at the start. It therefore draws the 3D axes unconditionally, encoding the incorrect assumption that visibility is not relevant or is handled elsewhere. The correct relationship is: the axes' draw method must only proceed when self.get_visible() is True (i.e., return early when False). 2) mpl_toolkits/mplot3d/axis3d.py: Axis.draw — The 3D Axis draw override similarly omits the visibility guard present in the base Axis.draw, encoding the incorrect assumption that axis-level visibility checks are unnecessary. The correct relationship is: Axis.draw must return early when self.get_visible() is False (and preserve the base-class behavior of clearing stale state). 3) The behavior is inconsistent with the base classes (matplotlib/axes/_base.py: Axes.draw and matplotlib/artist.py: Artist.set_visible), which do use/get the visibility flag; the 3D toolkit overrides break the Artist visibility contract by not respecting self.get_visible().
StepsToReproduce: Create a figure with two 3D subplots: fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, subplot_kw={'projection': '3d'})
Draw something on each axes: ax1.scatter(1,1,1); ax2.scatter(1,1,1, c='r')
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: After calling set_visible(False) on a 3D Axes, that subplot (the Axes and all its visible contents) is not drawn and does not appear in the displayed or saved figure.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The 3D subplot remains visible and its contents are rendered despite calling set_visible(False).

problem_statement.Title: Figure DPI doubles on each unpickle (MacOSX HiDPI / M1) leading to OverflowError
problem_statement.Description: When a Figure created on an HiDPI Mac (device_pixel_ratio > 1) is pickled and then unpickled, the figure.dpi value is multiplied by the device pixel ratio again on unpickle. Repeating pickle/unpickle doubles the DPI repeatedly and eventually causes an OverflowError in the macOS backend.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. Incorrect invariant in FigureCanvasBase.__init__ (lib/matplotlib/backend_bases.py): it unconditionally sets figure._original_dpi = figure.dpi when a canvas is (re)attached.

2. Broken interaction with pickling/unpickling (lib/matplotlib/figure.py: Figure.__setstate__): unpickling restores the figure.__dict__ (which may include a saved _original_dpi), then reattaches a canvas by calling FigureCanvasBase(self). Because FigureCanvasBase.__init__ overwrites the restored _original_dpi with the current figure.dpi, any saved logical (unscaled) DPI is lost.

3. HiDPI scaling logic assumes _original_dpi stores the logical (unscaled) DPI (backend_bases._set_device_pixel_ratio): it computes dpi = ratio * self.figure._original_dpi and calls figure._set_dpi(dpi). If _original_dpi was overwritten by an already-scaled value, this multiplies the DPI again, leading to doubling on each unpickle. This is the direct cause of the observed exponential DPI growth and eventual OverflowError when pixel dimensions exceed integer limits.

4. The concrete incorrect value/relationship: FigureCanvasBase.__init__ currently enforces the relationship _original_dpi == figure.dpi (unconditionally). The correct relationship is: _original_dpi should be the figure's logical/unscaled DPI and must not be overwritten if a saved _original_dpi is present after unpickling. Concretely, the code should only set _original_dpi when the Figure does not already have it (i.e., if not hasattr(figure, '_original_dpi')).
StepsToReproduce: Run the reproduction script from the original report on an M1 Mac with the MacOSX backend (Matplotlib 3.5.2 as in the report).
Observed behavior occurs when running a loop that pickles and unpickles the same Figure: the printed figure.dpi doubles after each unpickle and eventually raises OverflowError.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: After pickling and unpickling a Figure, the displayed/returned figure.dpi remains the same as before pickling (i.e., the logical DPI does not get multiplied again). Repeated pickle/unpickle cycles do not change the figure.dpi.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Each unpickle multiplies the figure.dpi by the device pixel ratio (on M1 Macs typically 2), causing the DPI to double on each iteration and eventually causing pixel dimension integers to overflow and raise an OverflowError in the backend.

problem_statement.Title: AttributeError: 'Poly3DCollection' object has no attribute '_facecolors2d' when calling get_facecolors()
problem_statement.Description: Calling get_facecolors() (or get_facecolor()) on the Poly3DCollection returned by plot_surface raises an AttributeError because the internal 2D color cache attribute _facecolors2d does not exist at the time of the call.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Poly3DCollection's get_facecolor/get_facecolors returns self._facecolors2d, but _facecolors2d is not guaranteed to be initialized during object construction or immediately after plot_surface returns. It is only created later in the 3D projection/initialization path (do_3d_projection / set_3d_properties). Location: lib/mpl_toolkits/mplot3d/art3d.py, Poly3DCollection.get_facecolor / (missing) get_facecolors and do_3d_projection / set_3d_properties.

2) The codebase assumes callers will not request 2D face/edge colors before the first draw/projection. That assumption is incorrect for legitimate user code that calls r.get_facecolors() immediately after plot_surface. Location: lib/mpl_toolkits/mplot3d/art3d.py, Poly3DCollection API surface.

3) There is an API-name ambiguity between plural and singular forms (get_facecolors vs get_facecolor) across layers/versions. Some callers invoke get_facecolors() while the 3D collection implementation exposes get_facecolor (singular) or vice versa, causing inconsistent behavior across installations. Location: lib/mpl_toolkits/mplot3d/art3d.py (Poly3DCollection) vs matplotlib.collections.PolyCollection.

4) The correct derived relationship that is expected but not always realized at call time: the 2D color cache _facecolors2d should be computed from the 3D-stored face color(s) and alpha using the color normalization function, i.e. _facecolors2d = mcolors.to_rgba_array( PolyCollection.get_facecolor(self), PolyCollection.get_alpha(self) ), yielding an (N,4) RGBA array. For edge colors, if the edge color is 'face', _edgecolors2d should reference _facecolors2d; otherwise _edgecolors2d = mcolors.to_rgba_array( PolyCollection.get_edgecolor(self), PolyCollection.get_alpha(self) ). If conversion cannot produce an array a safe fallback is an empty array with shape (0, 4). Location: lib/mpl_toolkits/mplot3d/art3d.py, set_3d_properties / do_3d_projection.
StepsToReproduce: Run a Python session with matplotlib (example tested on mpl 1.3.1 and 1.4.2).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Calling get_facecolors() (or get_facecolor()) on the Poly3DCollection returned by plot_surface should return an (N, 4) RGBA array (or a safe empty (0, 4) array) and should not raise an AttributeError, even if no draw/projection has occurred yet.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: An AttributeError is raised: "'Poly3DCollection' object has no attribute '_facecolors2d'" when calling r.get_facecolors() immediately after plot_surface.

The problem still is that we update some state during the draw process.  Internally we do the updating and the access in just the right order so that in most cases we do not notice that we also add the attributes on the fly. 

The best workaround for the current case (assuming 3.5):

```python

import matplotlib.tri as mtri
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from mpl_toolkits.mplot3d import Axes3D

y,x = np.ogrid[1:10:100j, 1:10:100j]

ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection='3d')

fig.draw_without_rendering()   # <--- This is the key line
r.get_facecolors()
```

However, I still think the best solution is https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/4067#issuecomment-73389124 which will require looking in `get_facecolors` to detect when these attributes are missing (and if stale?) and then force what ever needs to be done to update them.

Labeling this as a good first issue because it clearly has affected a lot of people for a long time, there is a very clear reproduction example (that will make a perfect test), and there are no API design choices to be made, but medium difficulty because it will require understanding some slightly tricking inheritance and how our 3D artists draw them selves.

problem_statement.Title: Persistent AttributeError: 'Line3D' object has no attribute '_verts3d' after failed 3D conversion
problem_statement.Description: After an earlier plotting call fails (e.g. due to incompatible numpy array shapes), some 2D artists are mutated to 3D classes but remain missing 3D-specific attributes. Later plotting attempts raise AttributeError: 'Line3D' object has no attribute '_verts3d' and the error persists until the Python kernel/process is restarted.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Conversion helper functions in lib/mpl_toolkits/mplot3d/art3d.py reassign an artist's __class__ to a 3D subclass (e.g. Line3D, Text3D, Patch3D, PathPatch3D, Line3DCollection, Path3DCollection, Patch3DCollection, Poly3DCollection) before performing the initialization/setter that creates the 3D-specific attributes. (Locations: art3d.py conversion functions text_2d_to_3d, line_2d_to_3d, line_collection_2d_to_3d, patch_2d_to_3d, pathpatch_2d_to_3d, patch_collection_2d_to_3d, poly_collection_2d_to_3d.)

2) If the subsequent setter (for example set_3d_properties or set_segments / set_verts_and_codes) raises (the investigation shows this can happen when numpy broadcasting fails — e.g. an error like "input operand has more dimensions than allowed by the axis remapping" from np.broadcast_to), the instance remains of the new 3D class but has not had its 3D attributes created. For Line3D the missing attribute observed is _verts3d (the investigation shows Line3D.__init__, set_data_3d and set_3d_properties are where _verts3d is supposed to be created). (Location: art3d.Line3D and its set_3d_properties / set_data_3d implementations.)

3) Because the mutation of the instance's __class__ is in-place and persists in-process, the partially-converted object remains in matplotlib's artist state across subsequent plotting calls in the same kernel/process. Later draws hit Line3D.draw which accesses self._verts3d and raise AttributeError: 'Line3D' object has no attribute '_verts3d' even after the original input data have been corrected. (Symptom source: persistent mutated artist instances in matplotlib state; stack trace shows mpl_toolkits.mplot3d.art3d.draw expecting _verts3d.)
StepsToReproduce: Create 2D data arrays with mismatched/incompatible shapes so that conversion to 3D triggers a broadcasting error (for example pass numpy arrays for zs with a shape that np.broadcast_to cannot match).
Run plotting code that triggers the 2D-to-3D conversion helpers (e.g. call ax.plot with arrays so line_2d_to_3d is used); observe the initial exception (e.g. a numpy broadcasting error).
Fix the input data (e.g. change the arrays to integers/scalars or otherwise valid shapes) and run the same plotting code again in the same Python process/kernel.
Observe that plt.show() (or figure draw) now raises AttributeError: 'Line3D' object has no attribute '_verts3d' and that this persists until you restart the Python kernel/process.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Plotting 3D lines should succeed when valid inputs are provided. If a prior plotting call raises due to invalid input, matplotlib should not leave artist objects in a partially-converted 3D state; subsequent valid plotting calls in the same process should work and display the expected 3D lines.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: After an earlier failure while converting 2D artists to 3D (for example a numpy broadcasting error), some artists remain in the 3D subclass but lack required 3D attributes (notably Line3D._verts3d). Subsequent plotting/draw attempts then raise AttributeError: 'Line3D' object has no attribute '_verts3d' and the problem persists in the running kernel until it is restarted.

problem_statement.Title: Legend constructor missing 'draggable' keyword (cannot enable dragging at creation)
problem_statement.Description: There is no constructor keyword to enable a legend's draggable behavior at creation time; users must call set_draggable(...) after creating a Legend.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) lib/matplotlib/legend.py:Legend.__init__ does not accept a 'draggable' keyword. This encodes the assumption that draggable state must be enabled via Legend.set_draggable(...) after construction rather than at creation time.

2) lib/matplotlib/legend.py:_legend_kw_doc (the legend keyword documentation) does not include a 'draggable' entry, encoding the omission in the public API/docs.

3) The presence of Legend.set_draggable(state, use_blit=False, update='loc') in lib/matplotlib/legend.py shows dragging support exists but is only exposed as a post-construction API, confirming the root cause is a missing constructor parameter rather than missing dragging functionality.
StepsToReproduce: Create a plot and a legend via an Axes: fig, ax = plt.subplots(); ax.plot([1,2,3], label='a')
Attempt to enable dragging at creation by calling ax.legend(draggable=True) or matplotlib.legend.Legend(..., draggable=True)
Observe that you cannot enable dragging at legend creation (constructor does not accept a 'draggable' keyword; dragging must be enabled by calling legend.set_draggable(...) after creation).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Passing draggable=True to Legend at creation time (e.g. ax.legend(draggable=True) or Legend(..., draggable=True)) should produce a legend that is immediately draggable with the mouse without requiring an extra call to set_draggable(...).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The Legend constructor does not accept a 'draggable' keyword; dragging can only be enabled by calling legend.set_draggable(...) after the Legend object has been created. The API and documentation do not provide a creation-time 'draggable' option.

problem_statement.Title: PostScript backend: TypeError when multi-line text contains an empty line
problem_statement.Description: Saving a figure with the PostScript backend raises a TypeError when the figure contains multi-line Text (title/label/annotation) that includes an empty line (a blank line break). The error occurs during text rendering in the PS backend.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. Incorrect assumption encoded in lib/matplotlib/backends/backend_ps.py (RendererPS.draw_text): the code assumes curr_stream will be a non-None list when the glyph-collection loop finishes. In the TTF (non-AFM) glyph-path the code unconditionally does stream.append(curr_stream) at the end, but curr_stream can be None.

2. Actual behavior of layout generator in lib/matplotlib/_text_helpers.py: _text_helpers.layout(s, font) yields no items for an empty input string (''), so the for-loop that sets curr_stream never runs and curr_stream remains None. This is a valid situation for blank lines produced by lib/matplotlib/text.py when splitting multi-line Text.

3. Consequence encoded at the same location (lib/matplotlib/backends/backend_ps.py, later in RendererPS.draw_text): stream may contain None elements. Later code expects each element of stream to be a (ps_name, xs_names) pair and does "for ps_name, xs_names in stream:", which fails with TypeError when encountering None.

4. Scope: this defect is specific to the TTF grouping code-path in RendererPS.draw_text. The AFM branch (ps.useafm True) always appends a (ps_name, xs_names) tuple (possibly with empty xs_names) and is not affected. The issue arises only when a line is empty and no glyphs are produced.

Run the script using the PostScript/EPS backend (e.g. default PS backend) on a Matplotlib build that exercises the TTF code-path (observed with Matplotlib 3.6.0).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The figure is saved as an EPS/PostScript file without raising an exception; empty lines in multi-line text are treated as vertical spacing and do not cause errors during rendering.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: A TypeError is raised when saving: "TypeError: cannot unpack non-iterable NoneType object". The exception occurs during text rendering in the PS backend (RendererPS.draw_text) while iterating 'for ps_name, xs_names in stream' because stream contains a None element for an empty text line.

problem_statement.Title: UserWarning about incompatible layout engine when constrained_layout=False
problem_statement.Description: Passing constrained_layout=False (or tight_layout=False) to Figure/pyplot causes a layout engine to be enabled anyway, which later triggers a UserWarning from subplots_adjust/tight_layout despite the user explicitly disabling constrained/tight layout.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Figure constructor treats explicit False as a non-None value and therefore enables layout engines. Location: lib/matplotlib/figure.py, class Figure, __init__().
   - Incorrect assumption: checking `tight_layout is not None` and `constrained_layout is not None` implies the caller requested that engine.
   - Concrete effect: calling plt.subplots(..., constrained_layout=False) enters the `constrained_layout is not None` branch and calls set_layout_engine(layout='constrained') even when the value is False.
   - Correct relationship: only enable the 'tight' or 'constrained' layout engine when the parameter is truthy (bool True) or when the parameter is a dict of parameters. If the parameter is explicitly False, do not enable the engine. If the parameter is a dict, enable the engine and apply .set(**dict).

2) The warning originates from FigureBase.subplots_adjust detecting a non-None layout engine that is incompatible with subplots_adjust. Location: lib/matplotlib/figure.py, class FigureBase, method subplots_adjust().
   - Condition used: if self.get_layout_engine() is not None and not self.get_layout_engine().adjust_compatible: warn(...).
   - How this ties to (1): because the constructor enabled the constrained engine when constrained_layout=False, get_layout_engine() returns a ConstrainedLayoutEngine (not None) and adjust_compatible is False, leading to the UserWarning.

3) pyplot.subplots forwards figure keyword arguments to Figure.__init__, so the user-level API passes the explicit False into the constructor unchanged. Location: matplotlib/pyplot.py, function subplots().
StepsToReproduce: Run the following Python code with matplotlib 3.6.0:
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: No UserWarning is emitted when constrained_layout=False (or tight_layout=False). subplots_adjust (and bbox_inches='tight') should be applied normally when the user has explicitly disabled constrained/tight layout.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: A UserWarning is emitted: "This figure was using a layout engine that is incompatible with subplots_adjust and/or tight_layout; not calling subplots_adjust." This occurs even though constrained_layout was passed as False and the user expected no constrained layout engine to be active.

problem_statement.Title: ax.bar raises StopIteration for all-NaN x values in matplotlib 3.6.1
problem_statement.Description: Calling ax.bar with an x sequence that contains no finite values (e.g. [np.nan]) raises StopIteration from deep inside Axes._convert_dx, causing plotting to fail (breaking callers such as seaborn's histogram phantom-bar behavior). In 3.6.0 the same call returned a BarContainer with a Rectangle whose x and/or height are NaN.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Assumption: callers of cbook._safe_first_finite will not raise StopIteration. Location: lib/matplotlib/axes/_axes.py, method Axes._convert_dx. Evidence: code calls cbook._safe_first_finite(x0) and cbook._safe_first_finite(xconv) inside try/except blocks that catch (TypeError, IndexError, KeyError) but not StopIteration; when x0 is [np.nan] _safe_first_finite raises StopIteration and it propagates out of _convert_dx.

2) Assumption: evaluating cbook._safe_first_finite repeatedly in boolean expressions is safe (won't raise). Location: lib/matplotlib/axes/_axes.py, function errorbar._upcast_err. Evidence: conditional uses isinstance(cbook._safe_first_finite(err), np.ndarray) (and later calls cbook._safe_first_finite(err) again) with no try/except; if err is iterable but contains no finite members, _safe_first_finite raises StopIteration and the expression propagates the exception.

3) Assumption: calls to cbook._safe_first_finite within inline isinstance checks are safe. Location: lib/matplotlib/axes/_axes.py, scatter/color-handling branch (detection of string color(s)). Evidence: expression isinstance(cbook._safe_first_finite(c), str) is evaluated without guarding StopIteration; when c is an iterable with no finite elements, _safe_first_finite raises StopIteration and scatter raises.

4) Incorrect handling of _safe_first_finite's contract. Root factual relationship: cbook._safe_first_finite yields the first finite element or raises StopIteration when no finite elements exist (e.g., input [np.nan]). Several call sites in axes/_axes.py assume a non-StopIteration outcome and therefore do not handle the StopIteration case; this mismatch between caller assumptions and _safe_first_finite's contract is the direct cause of the observed crash.
StepsToReproduce: python -c "import numpy as np; import matplotlib.pyplot as plt; f, ax = plt.subplots(); ax.bar([np.nan], [np.nan])"
Alternatively, in an interactive session: import numpy as np; import matplotlib.pyplot as plt; f, ax = plt.subplots(); ax.bar([np.nan], [0])
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Calling ax.bar with x and/or height sequences that contain only NaN values should not raise an exception. It should return a BarContainer (with Rectangle(s) whose x and/or height are NaN), as observed in matplotlib 3.6.0. High-level plotting code (e.g., seaborn histogram that draws/removes a phantom bar) should not be broken by such inputs.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: In matplotlib 3.6.1 ax.bar([np.nan], [np.nan]) raises StopIteration (traceback shows cbook._safe_first_finite causing StopIteration propagated out of Axes._convert_dx). ax.bar([np.nan], [0]) also raises; ax.bar([0], [np.nan]) continues to work. The unhandled StopIteration causes plotting to abort and higher-level code to fail.

problem_statement.Description: Direct lookup plt.style.library['seaborn-colorblind'] raises KeyError on Matplotlib 3.6.1, although style.use('seaborn-colorblind') still works and the same lookup succeeded on Matplotlib 3.4.3.
problem_statement.RootCause: 1) Base style files were renamed so their library keys are now 'seaborn-v0_8-...' rather than 'seaborn-...'. These keys come from reading BASE_LIBRARY_PATH via read_style_directory(...) into _base_library in lib/matplotlib/style/core.py. 2) style.use() implements a compatibility translation (inside style/core.py: the fix_style logic called by use()) by doing s = s.replace('seaborn', 'seaborn-v0_8') and issuing a deprecation warning; this allows style.use('seaborn-colorblind') to succeed by mapping the old name to the new key. 3) reload_library() in lib/matplotlib/style/core.py builds the final library with library = update_user_library(_base_library) and then sets available = sorted(library.keys()) but does not add deprecated 'seaborn-...' aliases. Because the legacy names are not present in style.library, direct dict access with a deprecated name (e.g. 'seaborn-colorblind') raises KeyError. 4) The correct relationship for backward compatibility is: for each key k in library where k.startswith('seaborn-v0_8'), create an alias name alias = k.replace('seaborn-v0_8', 'seaborn', 1) and have library[alias] refer to the same rc mapping (without overwriting any user-provided alias).

problem_statement.Title: Axis.set_ticks ignores and does not validate Text kwargs when labels is None (e.g. ax.set_xticks(..., xticklabels=...))
problem_statement.Description: When calling Axis.set_ticks (and the Axes.set_xticks wrapper) with Text-style kwargs but without passing labels, the kwargs are silently ignored and not validated. This allows typos or invalid keyword names (e.g. xticklabels) to fail silently.
problem_statement.RootCause: 1) Axis.set_ticks in lib/matplotlib/axis.py encodes the assumption that Text kwargs only matter when labels is provided. Its implementation calls set_ticklabels(labels, **kwargs) only if labels is not None; otherwise kwargs are ignored and never validated. 2) The Axes-level wrapper generated by _axis_method_wrapper in lib/matplotlib/axes/_base.py forwards extra kwargs (for example 'xticklabels') directly to Axis.set_ticks without mapping such caller-level aliases to the parameter name 'labels'. As a result, caller aliases like xticklabels arrive in Axis.set_ticks as ordinary kwargs. 3) Validation of Text properties currently happens when set_ticklabels invokes the Text validation path (Text._internal_update / Artist update). Because Axis.set_ticks skips calling set_ticklabels when labels is None, that validation path is not executed. The correct relationship is: kwargs that represent Text properties should be validated as Text properties regardless of whether labels is provided (i.e., Axis.set_ticks must validate kwargs as valid Text properties even when labels is None, using the existing Text validation mechanism).

Observe that no exception is raised and the xtick labels are not set (the provided 'xticklabels' kw was ignored)

problem_statement.Title: NumPy 1.24 DeprecationWarnings when colormap called with small unsigned integer arrays (matplotlib/colors.py)
problem_statement.Description: Calling a Matplotlib colormap with an integer array that has a small/unsigned dtype (e.g. np.uint8) produces NumPy 1.24 DeprecationWarnings from matplotlib/colors.py when the code assigns out-of-range Python integers into that array.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) lib/matplotlib/colors.py : Colormap.__call__ — Incorrect assumption that the user-provided index array xa (created via xa = np.array(X, copy=True)) has a dtype capable of holding sentinel index values. The method assigns sentinel integers into xa (xa[xa > self.N - 1] = self._i_over, xa[xa < 0] = self._i_under, xa[mask_bad] = self._i_bad) but does not promote small or unsigned integer dtypes. When xa.dtype is uint8 (or another small/unsigned integer dtype) these assignments use Python ints (examples from the warnings: 256, 257, 258) that overflow the array's dtype and trigger NumPy 1.24 deprecation warnings because NumPy will stop allowing out-of-range Python ints to be converted into narrow integer arrays.

2) lib/matplotlib/colors.py : Colormap.__call__ — Uneven handling of numeric kinds: the float code path converts floats to integer (xa.astype(int)) before sentinel assignment, which produces an integer array able to hold the sentinel values; the non-float (integer) code path leaves xa's original dtype unchanged. The implicit expectation encoded in the code is that non-float integer dtypes do not need promotion, which is false when the dtype is small/unsigned (e.g. uint8). The correct relationship (diagnostic) is: integer input arrays must be represented in a signed integer dtype with sufficient width to represent sentinel indices (i.e., values >= N and sentinel values such as N+1), for example a platform-sized signed integer (np.intp).
StepsToReproduce: Create a Python environment with Matplotlib (3.6.2) and NumPy 1.24+.
Observe DeprecationWarning messages from matplotlib/colors.py about conversion of 256/257/258 to uint8.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Calling a colormap with an integer array (including small/unsigned dtypes such as uint8) should not emit NumPy DeprecationWarnings. The colormap call should map values outside [0, N) to the over/under/bad entries without warnings.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: NumPy DeprecationWarning messages are emitted from lib/matplotlib/colors.py (around Colormap.__call__) complaining that conversion of out-of-bound Python integers (examples: 256, 257, 258) to uint8 will fail in the future. The warnings reference the three assignments that set xa[xa > self.N - 1], xa[xa < 0], and xa[mask_bad].

Thanks, I can now reproduce 😄 
The problem is that there are three more values, that are by default out of range in this case, to note specific cases:
https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/blob/8d2329ad89120410d7ef04faddba2c51db743b06/lib/matplotlib/colors.py#L673-L675
(N = 256 by default)

These are then assigned to out-of-range and masked/bad values here:
https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/blob/8d2329ad89120410d7ef04faddba2c51db743b06/lib/matplotlib/colors.py#L730-L732
which now raises a deprecation warning.

I think that one way forward would be to check the type of `xa` for int/uint and in that case take modulo the maximum value for `self._i_over` etc. This is basically what happens now anyway, but we need to do it explicitly rather than relying on numpy doing it. (I cannot really see how this makes sense from a color perspective, but we will at least keep the current behavior.)
I think this is exposing a real bug that we need to promote the input data to be bigger than uint8.  What we are doing here is buildin a lookup up table, the first N entries are for for values into the actually color map and then the next 3 entries are the special cases for over/under/bad so `xa` needs to be big enough to hold `self.N + 2` as values.
I don't know if this is a bigger bug or not, but I would like us to have fixed any deprecation warnings from dependencies before 3.7 is out (or if necessary a quick 3.7.1.)

problem_statement.Title: Setting LogNorm on an existing mappable/colorbar raises ValueError('Invalid vmin or vmax') (matplotlib 3.6.3)
problem_statement.Description: When a LogNorm is assigned to a mappable (e.g. QuadMesh from pcolormesh) after a Colorbar was created for that mappable, drawing can raise ValueError('Invalid vmin or vmax'). The error occurs during a redraw triggered by the norm change (interactive use), even when the data and the user-provided vmin/vmax are valid for LogNorm.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) lib/matplotlib/colors.py: Normalize/LogNorm runtime validity check - Norm.__call__ computes transformed endpoints and requires them to be finite: t_vmin, t_vmax = self._trf.transform([self.vmin, self.vmax]); if not np.isfinite([t_vmin, t_vmax]).all(): raise ValueError('Invalid vmin or vmax'). For LogNorm, this implies vmin and vmax must map to finite log values (vmin>0).  

2) lib/matplotlib/colorbar.py: Colorbar code mutates a shared Norm instance in-place while computing its mesh/boundaries (uses cbook._setattr_cm(self.norm, vmin=..., vmax=...) / setting self.norm.vmin = 0; self.norm.vmax = 1 when norm.scaled() is False). This encodes the assumption that temporarily changing the shared norm object is safe. That assumption is incorrect because the same Norm instance may be used concurrently by the mappable (collection/image). Temporarily setting vmin to 0 (or otherwise to values invalid for LogNorm) makes the LogTransform produce non-finite transformed endpoints and triggers the ValueError in Norm.__call__.  

3) lib/matplotlib/image.py: Image/resampling path also temporarily mutates the shared norm via the same pattern (cbook._setattr_cm(self.norm, vmin=..., vmax=...)) before calling the norm. The same incorrect assumption (that an in-place, temporary override of the shared norm cannot race with other users of the norm) is encoded here.  

Each of the above is equally relevant: colorbar/image temporarily overwrite the norm's vmin/vmax on the same Norm object that the mappable uses; Norm.__call__ treats transformed endpoints as invalid if they are non-finite (e.g. log(0) -> -inf) and raises ValueError, so transient in-place changes to vmin/vmax can make a valid LogNorm appear invalid during concurrent mapping.
StepsToReproduce: Run an interactive script that: 1) creates a 2D array with values in [1,5]; 2) plots it with ax.pcolormesh and attaches a colorbar (fig.colorbar(plot)); 3) shows the figure interactively (plt.ion()/fig.show()/plt.pause); 4) sets plot.norm = matplotlib.colors.LogNorm(1, 5) and calls plot.autoscale(); 5) observe the exception during the next draw.
Concretely, execute the provided reproduction script (the original report's code block) in matplotlib 3.6.3.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Assigning LogNorm(vmin, vmax) to an existing mappable should update the mappable and the colorbar and redraw without raising an exception. The colorbar and mappable should reflect the new LogNorm and render normally when vmin>0 and vmax>vmin.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: On assigning LogNorm after the colorbar exists and triggering a redraw, a ValueError is raised: "ValueError: Invalid vmin or vmax". The exception originates from colors.Normalize.__call__ when the norm's transform produces non-finite transformed endpoints (e.g. log(0) -> -inf). The traceback shows the error during the draw path (collections -> cm.to_rgba -> norm(x)).

problem_statement.Title: Pickling Figure fails when Legend/Annotation is draggable (TypeError: cannot pickle 'FigureCanvasQTAgg' object)
problem_statement.Description: Pickling a matplotlib Figure that contains a Legend or an Annotation/Text that has been made draggable raises a TypeError: cannot pickle 'FigureCanvasQTAgg' object. The error occurs when pickle tries to serialize runtime helper objects created to implement drag behavior.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Draggable helpers (DraggableLegend / DraggableAnnotation -> DraggableBase in offsetbox.py) store a direct reference to the figure's canvas (self.canvas = self.ref_artist.figure.canvas) and register callback ids on that canvas. These objects are runtime-only and hold unpicklable references to the backend-specific FigureCanvas (e.g., FigureCanvasQTAgg). Location: lib/matplotlib/offsetbox.py (DraggableBase and subclasses).

2) Legend objects keep their drag helper on Legend._draggable (set via Legend.set_draggable). Because Legend did not override __getstate__, pickling the Figure causes the pickler to descend into Legend._draggable and attempt to pickle the canvas referenced there, causing the TypeError. Location: lib/matplotlib/legend.py (Legend._draggable created by Legend.set_draggable).

3) Annotation/Text objects keep their drag helper on _draggable (set via Annotation.draggable / _AnnotationBase.draggable). Text.__getstate__ cleared only _renderer and did not remove _draggable, so pickling an annotation with an active draggable helper also leads the pickler to attempt serialization of the FigureCanvas. Location: lib/matplotlib/text.py (Text/Annotation._draggable).

4) The Figure.__getstate__ already excludes the Figure.canvas attribute from the Figure's own state, but it does not, and cannot, remove canvas references that are stored in arbitrary nested objects (like _draggable). The correct relationship for picklable state is that runtime-only canvas/callback registrations must not be serialized; they should be omitted from the serialized state of the owning Artist (for example, clearing _draggable).
StepsToReproduce: Create a figure and axes, plot something and add a legend.
Call leg.set_draggable(True) to make the legend draggable.
Attempt to pickle the figure, e.g. pickle.dumps(fig).
Observe that a TypeError is raised: cannot pickle 'FigureCanvasQTAgg' object.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Pickling a Figure that contains a Legend or an Annotation/Text should succeed (i.e., not raise TypeError) even if those artists were made draggable at runtime. Runtime-only canvas references and callback registrations should not block serialization of the Figure object.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Attempting to pickle a Figure with a draggable Legend or Annotation raises TypeError: cannot pickle 'FigureCanvasQTAgg' object because the draggable helper object (stored on the artist's _draggable attribute) holds a direct reference to the FigureCanvas and registered callbacks, which are not picklable.

problem_statement.Title: Clearing figure during RangeSlider.on_changed leaves canvas mouse grab / widgetlock referencing removed axes, blocking widget input
problem_statement.Description: When a RangeSlider callback (on_changed) calls pyplot.clf() while the slider still holds the mouse grab (i.e., during a drag), the figure clearing removes the axes but does not clear canvas-level state that references that axes. After redraw, newly created widgets cannot receive mouse input.
problem_statement.RootCause: 1) Figure.delaxes (lib/matplotlib/figure.py::Figure.delaxes) removes an Axes from the Figure but does not release the canvas mouse grab if the axis being removed currently owns it. The canvas.mouse_grabber therefore remains a stale reference to the removed Axes, preventing new widgets from obtaining the mouse grab (FigureCanvasBase.grab_mouse enforces that only None or the same ax may grab the mouse). 2) Figure.delaxes does not release a canvas.widgetlock owner when that owner is a widget tied to the axis being removed. If canvas.widgetlock._owner is a widget whose .ax is the axis being deleted, the lock can remain held by a stale owner and block other widgets/tools that rely on the widgetlock. (Both issues occur during the reproduction because RangeSlider triggers user callbacks while the slider holds the mouse grab; Button callbacks do not reproduce the issue because the Button handler releases the mouse grab before running observers.)
StepsToReproduce: In the RangeSlider's on_changed callback call pyplot.clf(), recreate widgets, and call pyplot.draw().
After the on_changed-triggered redraw, attempt to click/drag the widgets in the figure.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: After clearing and redrawing the figure inside a slider callback, the new widgets respond to mouse input (clicks/drags) the same as when the same sequence is executed from a button callback.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: After clearing and redrawing the figure inside a RangeSlider on_changed callback, widgets no longer receive mouse input (clicks/drags are ignored / interactivity is blocked).

I think what is happening is that because you are destroying the slider every time the you are also destroying the state of what value you changed it to.  When you create it you re-create it at the default value which produces the effect that you can not seem to change it.  Put another way, the state of what the current value of the slider is is in the `Slider` object.   Replacing the slider object with a new one (correctly) forgets the old value.

I agree it fails in surprising ways, but I think that this is best case "undefined behavior" and worst case incorrect usage of the tools.  In either case there is not much we can do upstream to address this (as if the user _did_ want to get fresh sliders that is not a case we should prevent from happening or warn on).
The "forgetting the value" is not the problem. My problem is, that the input just blocks for every widget in the figure. When that happens, you can click on any widget, but no callback gets triggered. That problem seems to happen when a callback of an object gets called that is/has been destroyed, but I don't know for sure.

But if that is from the incorrect usage of the tools, that's fine for me. I got a decent workaround that works currently, so I just have to keep that in my code for now.

problem_statement.Title: AttributeError in DraggableBase when using mplcursors + matplotlib 3.7.1: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'canvas'
problem_statement.Description: When using mplcursors with matplotlib 3.7.1, clicking a few data points causes an AttributeError ('NoneType' object has no attribute 'canvas') originating from matplotlib.offsetbox.DraggableBase. After this occurs, subsequent selections produce the same traceback.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) DraggableBase.canvas property assumes the artist and its figure are always present. Location: lib/matplotlib/offsetbox.py, DraggableBase.canvas defined as property(lambda self: self.ref_artist.figure.canvas). Incorrect assumption: evaluating self.ref_artist.figure.canvas without guarding can raise AttributeError when ref_artist or ref_artist.figure is absent.

2) _check_still_parented calls disconnect() when it detects self.ref_artist.figure is None. Location: lib/matplotlib/offsetbox.py, DraggableBase._check_still_parented. Incorrect assumption: calling disconnect() is safe even when the figure is gone; in the current code disconnect() itself dereferences the canvas.

3) disconnect dereferences the canvas via self.canvas.mpl_disconnect(...) while relying on the canvas property. Location: lib/matplotlib/offsetbox.py, DraggableBase.disconnect (and usages such as on_release which call self.canvas.mpl_disconnect(self._c1)). Incorrect assumption: disconnect can always obtain a live canvas by evaluating self.ref_artist.figure.canvas. Actual relationship required (diagnostic): disconnect must not dereference self.ref_artist.figure.canvas when the figure is None; canvas access must be guarded (or an already-obtained canvas reference must be used) because self.ref_artist.figure may be None during teardown, and dereferencing it raises AttributeError.
StepsToReproduce:
Save and run the provided script (Python 3.9, matplotlib 3.7.1, mplcursors installed):

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

x = np.arange(1, 11)
y1 = x

plt.scatter(x, y1)

mpl.cursor()
plt.show()
With the figure open, click several plotted points (pick events).
After a few clicks, observe the traceback in the terminal; further clicks trigger the same traceback.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Clicking points shows/updates the cursor/annotation without raising exceptions; repeated selections continue to work with no traceback printed to the terminal.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: After clicking a few points, a traceback is printed ending with AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'canvas' (originating from matplotlib.offsetbox property access). Subsequent point selections produce the same traceback and fail to operate normally.

problem_statement.Title: Colorbar update: replacing mappable.norm with LogNorm can leave vmin==0 and raise ZeroDivisionError
problem_statement.Description: After replacing a mappable's norm (for example setting plot.norm = LogNorm()) the colorbar is not updated correctly: cb.update_normal(plot) appears to have no effect, and invoking the brute-force update path (cb.update_bruteforce(plot) / draw_all -> _process_values) can raise a ZeroDivisionError originating in the norm inverse implementation.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Colorbar._process_values (matplotlib/colorbar.py : Colorbar._process_values) unconditionally sets an unscaled norm to vmin=0 and vmax=1 (the code path: "if not self.norm.scaled(): self.norm.vmin = 0; self.norm.vmax = 1"). This encodes the incorrect assumption that a default 0..1 range is valid for all norm types. That assumption is only valid for linear, zero-allowed norms. For log-like norms (LogNorm) vmin must be > 0; the correct relationship is that vmin/vmax should be derived from the mappable's data (e.g. via the mappable's autoscale/ autoscale_None behavior) when the norm is not already scaled. Evidence: colors.Normalize.inverse uses the formula "return vmin * ma.power((vmax / vmin), val)" (matplotlib/colors.py), which causes division-by-zero if vmin == 0.

2) Colorbar.update_normal (matplotlib/colorbar.py : Colorbar.update_normal) replaces self.norm with the mappable's new norm but does not attempt to initialize (autoscale) the new norm from the mappable's data before drawing. This encodes the incorrect assumption that a newly assigned norm with unset vmin/vmax can safely use default 0..1 values. The correct relationship is that when a user replaces the norm and that norm has unset vmin/vmax, the colorbar should initialize those bounds from the mappable's data (for example, by calling the ScalarMappable autoscale/autoscale_None path) so that non-linear norms (LogNorm) receive valid positive vmin/vmax.

3) The update_bruteforce -> draw_all -> _process_values code path (stack trace in the report: colorbar.py: update_bruteforce at ~line 967 -> draw_all at ~line 342 -> _process_values at ~line 664) assumes self.norm is already suitable for inverse/transform operations. If self.norm.vmin==0 (see item 1), calling norm.inverse (matplotlib/colors.py inverse implementation) leads to ZeroDivisionError. In short: a not-yet-initialized norm combined with a hardcoded fallback of 0..1 leads to invalid values for log-like norms and triggers the observed exception.
StepsToReproduce: Run the following Python snippet with matplotlib:
cb.update_bruteforce(plot)  # observed: raises ZeroDivisionError originating from colors.py inverse
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: After replacing the mappable's norm (e.g. setting plot.norm = LogNorm()) and updating the colorbar, the colorbar should reflect the new normalization (log scale) and its vmin/vmax should be appropriate for the mappable's data (no exception). Updating the colorbar should not raise exceptions even when the new norm requires positive vmin (LogNorm).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Calling cb.update_normal(plot) after assigning a new LogNorm has no visible effect on the colorbar. Calling cb.update_bruteforce(plot) (or otherwise forcing draw_all/_process_values) raises a ZeroDivisionError in matplotlib/colors.py when norm.inverse attempts to compute vmin * (vmax / vmin)**val because vmin == 0.

problem_statement.Title: xlim_changed not emitted on shared axis
problem_statement.Description: When two Axes share an x-axis (sharex=), callbacks registered on the sibling Axes (e.g. via ax.callbacks.connect('xlim_changed', ...)) are not called when the limits are changed via the other (source) Axes.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) lib/matplotlib/axes/_base.py : Axes.set_xlim delegates limit changes to the axis object, which results in sibling axes being updated via calls that suppress emission (the report shows other.set_xlim(..., emit=False)).

2) lib/matplotlib/axis.py : Axis._set_lim implements the emit logic. When emit is True it performs:
   - self.axes.callbacks.process(f"{name}lim_changed", self.axes)
   - then updates all siblings by calling other._axis_map[name]._set_lim(v0, v1, emit=False, auto=auto)
   Because siblings are updated with emit=False, their CallbackRegistry is not processed and their '<name>lim_changed' callbacks are not invoked. The code therefore only notifies the initiating axis's callbacks, not the callbacks of each axis whose view limits were changed.

3) Incorrect assumption encoded in Axis._set_lim: using emit=False when updating sibling axes both prevents recursive propagation (true) and also (unexpectedly) suppresses user-visible notifications on those sibling axes. The current implementation equates 'emit=False' with both 'suppress propagation' and 'suppress per-Axes callbacks', but users expect that every Axes whose limits change will receive its '<name>lim_changed' callbacks.

4) Practical constraint observed in the investigation: changing the sibling update to call sibling._set_lim(..., emit=True) re-enters propagation and can create recursion/cycles. This explains why the code uses emit=False when updating siblings, but it is the reason sibling callbacks are not invoked. The exact callback signal name used by the code is f"{name}lim_changed" and sibling updates are performed via other._axis_map[name]._set_lim(v0, v1, emit=False, auto=auto).
StepsToReproduce: Create two Axes that share the x-axis, e.g. ax1, ax2 = fig.subplots(2, sharex=True) or ax2 = fig.add_subplot(..., sharex=ax1).
Register callbacks on both axes: ax1.callbacks.connect('xlim_changed', cb1) and ax2.callbacks.connect('xlim_changed', cb2), where cbX records when it is called.
Call ax1.set_xlim(0, 1) (or otherwise change ax1 limits).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When an Axes' limits change (whether changed directly or via sharing), the '<name>lim_changed' callbacks registered on every Axes whose view limits were changed should be invoked so each Axes' callbacks observe the change.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Only the callbacks registered on the initiating Axes (the Axes on which set_xlim was called) are invoked. Callbacks registered on sibling/shared Axes are not called when their limits are changed via propagation from a shared axis.

```python
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# From https://matplotlib.org/stable/gallery/event_handling/resample.html
# A class that will downsample the data and recompute when zoomed.
class DataDisplayDownsampler:
    def __init__(self, xdata, ydata):
        self.origYData = ydata
        self.origXData = xdata
        self.max_points = 50
        self.delta = xdata[-1] - xdata[0]

    def downsample(self, xstart, xend):
        # get the points in the view range
        mask = (self.origXData > xstart) & (self.origXData < xend)
        # dilate the mask by one to catch the points just outside
        # of the view range to not truncate the line
        mask = np.convolve([1, 1, 1], mask, mode='same').astype(bool)
        # sort out how many points to drop
        ratio = max(np.sum(mask) // self.max_points, 1)

        # mask data
        xdata = self.origXData[mask]
        ydata = self.origYData[mask]

        # downsample data
        xdata = xdata[::ratio]
        ydata = ydata[::ratio]

        print("using {} of {} visible points".format(len(ydata), np.sum(mask)))

        return xdata, ydata

    def update(self, ax):
        # Update the line
        lims = ax.viewLim
        if abs(lims.width - self.delta) > 1e-8:
            self.delta = lims.width
            xstart, xend = lims.intervalx
            self.line.set_data(*self.downsample(xstart, xend))
            ax.figure.canvas.draw_idle()


# Create a signal
xdata = np.linspace(16, 365, (365-16)*4)
ydata = np.sin(2*np.pi*xdata/153) + np.cos(2*np.pi*xdata/127)


# --- This does not work: ax1 drawn after ax0 kills callbacks
d = DataDisplayDownsampler(xdata, ydata)
fig, (ax0, ax1) = plt.subplots(nrows=2, sharex=True)

# Hook up the line
d.line, = ax0.plot(xdata, ydata, 'o-')
ax0.set_autoscale_on(False)  # Otherwise, infinite loop

# Connect for changing the view limits
ax0.callbacks.connect('xlim_changed', d.update)
ax0.set_xlim(16, 365)

ax1.plot(xdata, -ydata)
plt.show()


# --- This does work: ax0 drawn after ax1
# --- Note: only works if axis limits are controlled via ax0, not ax1
# Create a signal
xdata = np.linspace(16, 365, (365-16)*4)
ydata = np.sin(2*np.pi*xdata/153) + np.cos(2*np.pi*xdata/127)

d = DataDisplayDownsampler(xdata, ydata)

fig, (ax0, ax1) = plt.subplots(nrows=2, sharex=True)

ax1.plot(xdata, -ydata)

# Hook up the line
d.line, = ax0.plot(xdata, ydata, 'o-')
ax0.set_autoscale_on(False)  # Otherwise, infinite loop

# Connect for changing the view limits
ax0.callbacks.connect('xlim_changed', d.update)
ax0.set_xlim(16, 365)


plt.show()

```

which doesn't do the `ax0.callbacks.process('xlim_changed', self)` 

If we don't do this, it continues to emit to the shared axes and we get an infinite recursion.  

Something like

```py
from typing import Any

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib.axes import Axes


def add_identity(ax: Axes = None, **line_kwargs: Any) -> None:
    """Add a parity line (y = x) to the provided axis."""
    if ax is None:
        ax = plt.gca()

    # zorder=0 ensures other plotted data displays on top of line
    default_kwargs = dict(alpha=0.5, zorder=0, linestyle="dashed", color="black")
    (identity,) = ax.plot([], [], **default_kwargs, **line_kwargs)

    def callback(axes: Axes) -> None:
        x_min, x_max = axes.get_xlim()
        y_min, y_max = axes.get_ylim()
        low = max(x_min, y_min)
        high = min(x_max, y_max)
        identity.set_data([low, high], [low, high])

    callback(ax)
    # Register callbacks to update identity line when moving plots in interactive
    # mode to ensure line always extend to plot edges.
    ax.callbacks.connect("xlim_changed", callback)
    ax.callbacks.connect("ylim_changed", callback)


fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, sharex=True, sharey=True)

ax1.plot([0, 1], [1, 0])
add_identity(ax1)

ax2.plot([0, 2], [2, 0])
add_identity(ax2)

plt.savefig('tmp.png')
```

problem_statement.Title: TypeError when creating AxesGrid with non-default axis class (e.g. cartopy GeoAxes)
problem_statement.Description: Creating an AxesGrid with a non-default axes_class (for example cartopy.mpl.geoaxes.GeoAxes passed to axes_grid1.AxesGrid) raises TypeError: 'method' object is not subscriptable during Grid initialization. The error occurs when AxesGrid code tries to index ax.axis as if it were a mapping of axis artists.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect assumption in mpl_toolkits/axes_grid1/axes_grid.py::_tick_only that ax.axis is subscriptable/mapping-like and returns objects with .toggle(...). The code uses bottom_off = not bottom_on and left_off = not left_on and then calls ax.axis["bottom"].toggle(ticklabels=bottom_off, label=bottom_off) and ax.axis["left"].toggle(ticklabels=left_off, label=left_off). This assumption only holds for the axes_grid1-provided mpl_axes.Axes (which exposes an AxisDict via its axis property).

2) Same incorrect assumption in mpl_toolkits/axes_grid1/axes_grid.py::CbarAxesBase.toggle_label where self.axis[self.orientation].toggle(ticklabels=b, label=b) is used. That code assumes axis[...] exists and supports .toggle(...).

3) In contrast, many third-party Axes subclasses (e.g. cartopy.mpl.geoaxes.GeoAxes) inherit the normal Matplotlib Axes where ax.axis is the Axes.axis callable/method (used to get/set limits or 'off') and is not subscriptable. When such an axes_class is used, indexing ax.axis[...] raises TypeError: 'method' object is not subscriptable. The library's mpl_toolkits.axes_grid1.mpl_axes.Axes implements a property axis that returns an AxisDict (which is subscriptable and provides .toggle), explaining why the code worked for the toolkit's default axes but fails for non-default axes.
StepsToReproduce: Install cartopy and matplotlib (the report used Matplotlib 3.1.2, cartopy 0.17.0).
Run a short script that creates an AxesGrid with cartopy GeoAxes as axes_class, for example:
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: AxesGrid initializes successfully when given a non-default axes_class (such as cartopy GeoAxes). The grid is created and axes appear; label/tick visibility is set appropriately according to the grid's label_mode without raising exceptions.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: During AxesGrid initialization a TypeError is raised: "TypeError: 'method' object is not subscriptable". The exception originates from mpl_toolkits/axes_grid1/axes_grid.py in _tick_only when the code attempts to evaluate ax.axis["bottom"] (and/or ax.axis["left"]). The stack trace in the report shows the error occurs while set_label_mode calls _tick_only.

I agree with @anntzer comment above (https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/17069#issuecomment-611635018), but feel that we should move axes_grid into core matplotlib and change the API as we see fit when we do so.
